dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at [CITY], room 116.
Hello ma'am, this is Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. We received a report of difficulty breathing. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having shortness of breath for quite a while now.
Joan, I need to test your level of responsiveness. First, I'll tell you to open your eyes.
Eyes are tracking, and she's alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Ma'am, we're going to suction your tracheostomy to help with your breathing.
We suctioned your tracheostomy to help clear any blockage. That should make it easier for you to breathe.
Ma'am, we're going to check your blood pressure now.
Your blood pressure is 139 over 85. Would you mind if I check your pulse?
Your pulse is 93 beats per minute. I'll check your breathing now.
You're breathing at 13 breaths per minute. Let's check your oxygen saturation.
Pulse oximeter reads 89% currently. That's slightly low. We'll provide supplemental oxygen right away.
We're using a pediatric non-rebreather to give you 8 liters of oxygen. You may feel a bit cool or dry.
Let me listen to your lungs. We have decreased breath sounds on your left side but normal sounds on your right.
We're applying a cardiac monitor to keep track of your heart rhythm.
We'll use 4 lead ECG monitoring for continuous surveillance of your heart.
Your pulse has increased slightly to 99 now. We'll monitor it closely.
You're breathing at 18 breaths per minute now, and your oxygen saturation is 96%. That's a positive sign.
Your blood pressure is now 135 over 80. Other than that, there's no reported pain, and your glucose levels remain stable.
You pointed at your tracheostomy collar earlier. Was your shortness of breath associated with it?
I've reviewed the chart. You have a history of primary hypertension, osteoarthritis, ovarian cancer, paraplegia, non-insulin-dependent diabetes, and epilepsy. Is that correct?
You also mentioned the staff at [CITY] hasn't suctioned you regularly. We'll bring that to the hospital's attention.
You remain alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-A&O x4.
The monitor is showing a normal sinus rhythm. Suctioning seems to have improved your breathing.
Joan is secured on the stretcher, and we are preparing to move to the ambulance.
All bags and gear are in place. We're ready for transport now.
We are en route now. The emergency room at MCV is expecting you.
The patient remained alert and oriented throughout our care and transport.
She expressed concern about suctioning and requested a different nursing home.
We followed Protocol 3-11 for Medical - Respiratory Distress.
Her belongings remain at [CITY]. We'll inform the receiving nurse during handover.
All straps and transport lines secured.
MCV will be the destination based on patient's last tracheostomy placement per statement.
Blood pressure remains 135 over 80. Otherwise, vitals are stable.
Pulse is stable at 99. Respirations are 18, oxygen saturation is 96%.
Breathing appears unlabored, and lung sounds are still clear on the right, decreased on the left.
Oxygen delivery via pediatric NRB is uninterrupted at 8 liters per minute.
ECG rhythm still shows sinus. No significant arrhythmia noted.
We repeated the monitoring to confirm stability. No changes from the last reading.
Joan, can you tell us if your breathing has changed again?
Still a bit tight in my chest but noticeably better than before.
I re-reviewed her history: no reported medication allergies.
Her medications include Albuterol, Levetiracetam, and Apixaban, among others. No recent intake of concern reported.
Eyes tracked normally, speech and motor function appear intact.
She initially requested suctioning directly, no other prior treatment given at the scene.
Appearance of skin seems normal. Nothing alarming noted.
Pupils equal and reactive.
No foreign bodies, obstruction or drainage observed from the airway.
No rib deformity or trauma observed. Symmetrical chest movements.
Exam not conducted due to stable condition and no signs of acute abdomen.
MCV is the chosen facility, as per patient's preference.
All equipment and medications are secured for transport.
All vitals remain stable at previous readings during transport.
Re-check of monitors: no change in pattern.
Transporting via ambulance stretcher.
No signs of pain reported by patient during assessment. Remains verbalized pain 0.
Patient reclined in supine position during transport with no complaints.
No delays or changes noted during transfer to ambulance.
Blood pressure again confirmed at 135/80. No concern for hypotension.
Heart rate unchanged at 99. No signs of tachycardia.
Respiration rate remains 18, without labored efforts.
Transport continues uneventfully. Communication with ER established.
Rhythm still shows sinus. No acute changes.
Patient remained alert and oriented throughout transport.
Patient securely on stretcher and transferred to transporter without incident.
All straps and transport lines secured.
Ambulance approaching MCV ER on stretcher delivery route.
All medications and documentation on hand for receivables.
Patient ready for nursing handoff. Present condition stable.
Verbal and written reports completed and ready.
Transferring to MCV receiving RN after full documentation.
Monitored no delays in receiving care. Documentation assigned.
Patient awake, alert, and oriented during all phases.
All procedures complete during transit to the hospital.
Summary: Presenting complaint of shortness of breath. Patient alert and oriented.
All vital readings stable throughout management.
Emergency facility accepted patient for further evaluation.
Movement confirmed as per protocol to ER.
Transfer finalized at MCV ER with RN. All reports delivered hands-on.
Given no additional pressures or non-trivial information from [CITY].
Patient placed in ambulance without complications during handling.
Departed on time as scheduled for patient maintenance.
Anticipated arrival within normal transport times outlined.
Transfer accomplished on time without difficulty.
Full verbal report given to receiving RN.
Time to document and finalize the call now.
Patient delivered to hospital with all supporting reports.
Patient was transported to [COMPANY_NAME] System Cold.
Patient is awake during all transitions.
No equipment or stretcher damage observed en route.
Patient handover confirmed, ready for departure.
All movement completed using two-person lift to stretcher.
Transition to stretcher completed safely.
Complete documentation on file accordingly.
Patient's personal items were left at their room.
No personal effects transported due to procedures unavailable.
Case closed per procedure at the facility.
All documents are ready for filing process.
No outstanding issues or emergent requirements detected.
Per protocol 3-11.
